title: Perspective Transformations
concept: A perspective transformation warps an image to simulate viewing it from a different angle, and unlike affine transforms it can make parallel lines converge or diverge — the key to tasks like flattening a photographed document.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can't an affine transform fix a photo where parallel edges visibly converge toward one side?

options.text: Affine transforms are mathematically forced to keep parallel lines parallel, so they can't make converging lines spread apart
Affine transforms can only be used on scanned text, never on photos
Affine transforms always shrink the image

exercises.explain: Right — affine can't touch how lines converge. That's exactly the gap perspective transformations fill.

cards.title: Straightening a photographed document
cards.body: Take a photo of a receipt or a page lying flat on a table, shot from a slight angle instead of straight overhead. The rectangle looks like a lopsided trapezoid in your photo — yet scanner apps can flatten it back into a perfect rectangle, as if you'd photographed it from directly above.

cards.title: What is a perspective transformation?
cards.body: A perspective transformation warps an image by mapping four corner points to a new set of four points, simulating a view from a different camera angle. Unlike affine transforms, it can make parallel lines converge or diverge — which is exactly what's needed to correct (or add) the illusion of depth and viewing angle.

cards.title: Mentally straightening a door seen from the side
cards.body: Stand off to the side of an open rectangular door and look at it — it appears trapezoidal, narrower on the far edge. Your brain still knows it's a rectangle and can imagine the straight-on view. A perspective transform does that same "imagine it from a different angle" math directly on the pixels.

cards.title: How a scanning app finds and flattens a page
cards.body: A phone scanning app detects the four corners of a document in the photo, then computes a perspective transform that stretches those four corners into a perfect rectangle — pulling the near edge in and the far edge out until the whole page looks like it was shot flat-on.

cards.title: Perspective isn't just a stronger affine transform
cards.body: It's easy to assume perspective transforms are simply affine transforms turned up a notch. They're actually a different category: affine transforms are defined by 3 point correspondences and always preserve parallel lines; perspective transforms use 4 point correspondences, which is precisely the extra freedom needed to bend parallel lines toward or away from each other.

cards.title: Affine vs. perspective, side by side
cards.body: The one capability that separates them:

left.text:
3 reference points
Parallel lines always stay parallel

right.text:
4 reference points
Parallel lines can converge or diverge

exercises.prompt: A scanning app flattens a photo of a tilted document into a straight-on rectangle. Which transform is doing this?
exercises.explain: Correct — making converging edges spread back into a rectangle requires a perspective transform, not just affine moves.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A perspective transformation can make lines that were parallel in the real world appear to converge or diverge.
exercises.explain: True. That's precisely the extra flexibility perspective transforms have over affine transforms.

exercises.sentence: Perspective transforms map four ___ points to a new quadrilateral, allowing parallel lines to converge or diverge.
exercises.explain: Corner is correct — four corner correspondences give perspective transforms the freedom affine transforms lack.

exercises.prompt: Which task specifically requires a perspective transform rather than a simple affine one?

options.text: Turning a photo of a whiteboard shot at a steep angle into a flat, straight-on rectangle
Rotating an already-level scanned page by 2° to straighten it
Shifting an image 10 pixels to the left

exercises.explain: Correcting a steep viewing angle requires bending converging lines back apart — a job only perspective transforms can do.

exercises.prompt: Across translation, rotation, scaling, cropping, resizing, affine, and perspective, what single idea unifies all of these geometric transforms?

options.text: Each one repositions or resamples the pixels of an image according to a defined geometric rule, without using any learned parameters
Each one requires training a neural network on millions of labeled images first
Each one only works on images that have already been converted to grayscale
Each one permanently deletes color information from the image

exercises.explain: Exactly — every transform in this unit is a fixed geometric rule for moving or resampling pixels, no learning involved. Next: why this exact toolset is the secret behind one of deep learning's most important training tricks.
